claim_text: As the aforementioned metrics were rigorously assessed in the Mason study,6 we include only the best overall performing metric (VIF) along with the most commonly used metrics (NRMSE, SSIM, PSNR) in our subsequent analyses.

sub_claims.text: Mason 2020 was a rigorous assessment of IQ (image quality) metrics.

evidence.snippet: The comparison uses a database of MR images of the brain and abdomen that have been retrospectively degraded by noise, blurring, undersampling, motion, and wavelet compression for a total of 414 degraded images. A total of 1017 subjective scores were assigned by five radiologists. IQM performance was measured via the Spearman rank order correlation coefficient (SROCC) and statistically significant differences in the residuals of the IQM scores and radiologists' scores were tested.

Three body radiologists and two neuro radiologists were involved in the study. The radiologists scored only images from their subspecialties, using a 1-5 Likert scale. ... This scale was calibrated by consensus of radiologists in each subspecialty based on a training set consisting of three reference MR images (different from those in the testing set) and degraded images generated by applying each degradation technique to each reference at two representative strengths.

The Spearman rank order correlation coefficient (SROCC) was calculated between the transformed radiologist scores and each of the IQM scores. ... A higher SROCC would then correspond to a better performing IQM.

A variance-based hypothesis test was performed to measure statistical significance in the difference in the performance of the IQMs. ... To test for statistical differences in the variance of residuals an F-test of equality of variances was performed, with the null hypothesis being that the residuals of two IQMs come from distributions of equal variance (with 95% confidence). Since each IQM was compared to all nine other IQMs, a Benjamini-Hochberg correction for false discovery rate controlling was performed.

TABLE III WEIGHTED COHEN'S KAPPA FOR SCORES BETWEEN RADIOLOGISTS RATING THE SAME SET OF IMAGES ... The weighted kappa between radiologists who rated the same set of images is presented in Table III. Moderate to substantial agreement was found.
We measured the correlations between 10 full-reference objective IQMs and the subjective image quality score of five subspecialty radiologists.

sub_claims.nuance: Mason 2020 evaluated 10 full-reference IQMs against 1017 subspecialty-radiologist scores over 414 degraded images, with calibration training set, Cohen's kappa inter-rater check, SROCC, F-test on residual variances with Benjamini-Hochberg correction, and Gaussianity verification via kurtosis -- a methodologically thorough reader study.

sub_claims.text: VIF was the best overall performing metric per Mason 2020.

evidence.snippet: The SROCC of each IQM with each radiologist's scores are shown in Fig. 4. ... Note that RMSE and SSIM are among the metrics with the lowest SROCC. Overall, VIF had the highest SROCC values. Results of the hypothesis testing on the variance in the residuals for each IQM in for each radiologist are shown in Table IV. The sorted IQMs show that for many of the radiologists in the study, VIF, FSIM, and NQM perform statistically better than the other IQMs included in the study.

After normalizing each radiologist's score and combining scores across all images, we found that VIF exhibited the highest SROCC of all the metrics evaluated in this study. These results suggest that VIF provides the most accurate surrogate measure of subjective image quality scores of a radiologist of the IQMs included in this study.

FSIM and NQM also consistently demonstrated high correlations with the radiologist scores. Indeed, NQM had performed statistically better than VIF for images degraded with Gaussian noise, undersampling, or wavelet compression.
The VIF, FSIM, and NQM demonstrated the highest correlation with radiologists' opinions of MR image quality.

sub_claims.nuance: Mason explicitly states VIF had the highest overall SROCC and was the most accurate surrogate measure overall; however, NQM was statistically better than VIF on three specific degradation subsets (Gaussian noise, undersampling, wavelet compression), and the conclusion groups VIF/FSIM/NQM together as 'highest'. The manuscript's 'best overall performing metric (VIF)' framing matches the overall/combined finding but elides the per-degradation nuance.

attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Mason 2020 reports its own primary study (414 retrospectively degraded images, 1017 subspecialty-radiologist scores, 10 IQMs with SROCC + F-test + Benjamini-Hochberg). The VIF-as-best finding and the rigorous-assessment characterization are both attributable to Mason directly, not to a cited primary.
attestation.out_of_context_check: Mason's 'overall' VIF-best result combines all radiologists across all images; it does not hold per-degradation (NQM beats VIF on Gaussian noise, undersampling, wavelet compression per L33). The manuscript's phrase 'best overall performing metric (VIF)' aligns with Mason's combined-data framing. No materially different context detected, but adjudicator may want to consider whether 'best overall' is precise enough given the per-degradation reversal.
